architecture.summary: This paper evaluates existing model editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, MEND) applied sequentially to the same language model. These methods localize facts to specific layers via causal tracing, then update weights of identified layers to inject new knowledge. The paper does not propose a new architecture but rather performs systematic empirical evaluation of how these methods degrade under repeated sequential editing, measuring editing proficiency, fact forgetting, and downstream task performance as edits accumulate.

biblio.title: Model Editing at Scale leads to Gradual and Catastrophic Forgetting

claims.statement: As a model is edited sequentially with multiple facts, it continually forgets previously edited facts and the ability to perform downstream tasks, happening in two phases—an initial gradual but progressive forgetting phase followed by abrupt or catastrophic forgetting phase.
For ROME, the efficacy score is almost 100% until a point where it begins to decline, which can come as early as 100 edits or as late as 1000 edits.
MEMIT forgets almost three times fewer facts compared to ROME when performing sequential edits.

Edits made by ROME and MEMIT are not as localized as previously believed; new edits consistently bleed into other facts stored in the model, as evidenced by neighborhood score consistently declining with additional edits.
Disabling edits in ROME have normalized L2 distance from original weights three orders of magnitude larger than non-disabling edits.
MEND is unable to make multiple sequential edits to the same model, as shown by its efficacy score, reiterating previous observations that MEND cannot be used to reliably edit knowledge at scale.

Catastrophic forgetting in ROME occurs when the edited layer becomes so different from its original version that compatibility with other transformer layers is destroyed, causing the model to catastrophically forget previously edited facts and lose ability to perform downstream tasks.

MEMIT maintains ability to perform downstream tasks on GPT-J as more edits are made, but shows gradual loss of downstream performance on GPT2-XL with multiple edits, with the model losing ability on paraphrase detection long before catastrophic forgetting point.
